title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Karlan and List (2007) study whether matching grants increase charitable donations, and whether larger match ratios generate even more giving. They partnered with a large nonprofit organization that sent direct mail solicitations to over 50,000 prior donors. Individuals were randomly assigned to either a control group (standard letter) or a treatment group (letter announcing a matching grant). Within the treatment group, the match ratio was further randomized across three levels: 1:1, 2:1, and 3:1.
Because treatment was randomly assigned, any difference in donation outcomes between groups can be attributed causally to the matching offer itself, rather than to pre-existing differences among donors. This is the core advantage of a randomized experiment over observational data.
The key findings are that simply announcing a match significantly increases both the probability of donating and the revenue per solicitation. However, larger match ratios (2:1 or 3:1) have no additional effect relative to a simple 1:1 match — a surprising result with practical implications for fundraisers.
In this post, I replicate Table 1, Table 2A (Panel A), and Table 3 from the paper, and then visualize the main treatment effects as a chart.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Karlan and List (2007) study whether matching grants increase charitable donations, and whether larger match ratios generate even more giving. They partnered with a large nonprofit organization that sent direct mail solicitations to over 50,000 prior donors. Individuals were randomly assigned to either a control group (standard letter) or a treatment group (letter announcing a matching grant). Within the treatment group, the match ratio was further randomized across three levels: 1:1, 2:1, and 3:1.
Because treatment was randomly assigned, any difference in donation outcomes between groups can be attributed causally to the matching offer itself, rather than to pre-existing differences among donors. This is the core advantage of a randomized experiment over observational data.
The key findings are that simply announcing a match significantly increases both the probability of donating and the revenue per solicitation. However, larger match ratios (2:1 or 3:1) have no additional effect relative to a simple 1:1 match — a surprising result with practical implications for fundraisers.
In this post, I replicate Table 1, Table 2A (Panel A), and Table 3 from the paper, and then visualize the main treatment effects as a chart.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Main Analysis

Data
The dataset contains 50,083 observations, each representing a prior donor who received a solicitation letter.

df = pd.read_stata("AERtables1-5.dta")
df = df[df['treatment'].notna()]

print(f"Total observations: {len(df):,}")
print(f"Treatment group:    {int((df['treatment']==1).sum()):,}")
print(f"Control group:      {int((df['control']==1).sum()):,}")

Total observations: 50,083
Treatment group:    33,396
Control group:      16,687




Balance Check (Table 1)
A well-designed randomized experiment should produce treatment and control groups that look similar on observable characteristics. Table 1 in the paper confirms this. I replicate the key rows below. I do not report formal t-tests here, but the descriptive balance is very close to the published table.

balance_vars = {
    'MRM2': 'Months since last donation',
    'HPA': 'Highest previous contribution',
    'freq': 'Number of prior donations',
    'years': 'Years since initial donation',
    'dormant': 'Donor activity flag (dormant)',
    'female': 'Female',
    'couple': 'Couple',
}

treatment = df[df['treatment'] == 1]
control = df[df['control'] == 1]

rows = []
for var, label in balance_vars.items():
    rows.append({
        'Variable': label,
        'All (Mean)': f"{df[var].mean():.3f}",
        'All (SD)': f"({df[var].std():.3f})",
        'Treatment (Mean)': f"{treatment[var].mean():.3f}",
        'Treatment (SD)': f"({treatment[var].std():.3f})",
        'Control (Mean)': f"{control[var].mean():.3f}",
        'Control (SD)': f"({control[var].std():.3f})",
    })

rows.append({
    'Variable': 'Observations',
    'All (Mean)': f"{len(df):,}",
    'All (SD)': '',
    'Treatment (Mean)': f"{len(treatment):,}",
    'Treatment (SD)': '',
    'Control (Mean)': f"{len(control):,}",
    'Control (SD)': '',
})

table1 = pd.DataFrame(rows)
table1

The means are nearly identical across the treatment and control groups for every variable, confirming that randomization was successful. Any differences in donation behavior we observe can be attributed to the treatment rather than pre-existing differences between groups.


Experimental Results (Table 2A, Panel A)
Table 2A presents the core experimental results. I compute three key outcome measures across the control group, the full treatment group, and the three match-ratio subgroups:

groups = {
    'Control': df[df['control'] == 1],
    'Treatment': df[df['treatment'] == 1],
    '1:1 Match': df[(df['treatment'] == 1) & (df['ratio'] == 1)],
    '2:1 Match': df[(df['treatment'] == 1) & (df['ratio'] == 2)],
    '3:1 Match': df[(df['treatment'] == 1) & (df['ratio'] == 3)],
}

rows = []
for name, g in groups.items():
    n = len(g)
    resp = g['gave'].mean()
    resp_se = g['gave'].std() / np.sqrt(n)
    uncond = g['amount'].mean()
    uncond_se = g['amount'].std() / np.sqrt(n)
    givers = g[g['gave'] == 1]
    cond = givers['amount'].mean() if len(givers) > 0 else 0
    cond_se = givers['amount'].std() / np.sqrt(len(givers)) if len(givers) > 0 else 0

    rows.append({
        'Group': name,
        'Response Rate': f"{resp:.3f} ({resp_se:.3f})",
        'Unconditional $': f"{uncond:.3f} ({uncond_se:.3f})",
        'Conditional $': f"{cond:.3f} ({cond_se:.3f})",
        'N': f"{n:,}",
    })

table2a = pd.DataFrame(rows)
table2a

These values match the published Table 2A very closely. The control group response rate is 0.018, while the treatment group’s is 0.022 — a relative increase of about 22%. Unconditional giving is also higher in the treatment group ($0.967 vs. $0.813). Notably, the three match ratios produce very similar outcomes: increasing the match from 1:1 to 3:1 does not meaningfully boost donations.


Regression Analysis (Table 3)
To formally test these patterns, I estimate linear probability models with the binary donation indicator (gave) as the dependent variable. The paper labels these as probit regressions, but the coefficients are consistent with OLS estimates, as noted in the assignment instructions.
I replicate the main treatment-effect regressions for the full sample and for the 2005 donation-status subsamples using linear probability models. The estimated treatment effects closely match the magnitudes reported in Table 3.

# Column 1: Treatment effect on full sample
y = df['gave'].values
X1 = sm.add_constant(df[['treatment']].values)
m1 = sm.OLS(y, X1).fit()

# Column 3: Already gave in 2005 (dormant==1, n=26,217 matches paper)
gave_2005 = df[df['dormant'] == 1]
X3 = sm.add_constant(gave_2005[['treatment']].values)
m3 = sm.OLS(gave_2005['gave'].values, X3).fit()

# Column 5: Had not yet given in 2005 (dormant==0, n=23,866 matches paper)
not_gave = df[df['dormant'] == 0]
X5 = sm.add_constant(not_gave[['treatment']].values)
m5 = sm.OLS(not_gave['gave'].values, X5).fit()

The coefficient in column 1 is 0.0042, matching the paper’s reported value of 0.004. This means the matching offer increases the probability of donating by about 0.4 percentage points. Given a baseline rate of 1.8%, this is a roughly 22% relative increase.
Columns 3 and 5 show that the treatment effect is larger for donors who had not yet given in 2005 (0.0054) compared to those who already had (0.0031), suggesting that the match is somewhat more effective at reactivating lapsed donors. These estimates and sample sizes match the paper closely.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Something Additional

Visualizing Treatment Effects
The paper presents all its results in tables. To make the key finding more visually intuitive, I plot the response rates and average gift amounts across the control and treatment subgroups:





Donation Outcomes by Experimental Group




The visual pattern is clear. All three treatment groups show a lift in both response rate and average giving relative to the control group. But the three match ratios produce bars of essentially the same height, confirming that the existence of a match matters more than its generosity.


Why Randomization Supports a Causal Interpretation
An important feature of this study is that it uses a randomized field experiment rather than observational data. Because individuals were randomly assigned to treatment and control, the two groups are comparable in expectation on both observed and unobserved characteristics. The balance check in Table 1 confirms that this holds in the sample.
This means we can interpret the difference in donation rates between the treatment and control groups as the causal effect of receiving a matching offer. With observational data, there would always be a concern that donors who received a match differed from those who did not in ways that also affected their likelihood of giving (selection bias). Randomization eliminates this concern by design.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Conclusion
This replication confirms the central findings of Karlan and List (2007). Matching grants increase the probability of giving and raise average dollars per solicitation. However, the magnitude of the match ratio has no additional effect — a result that challenges conventional fundraising wisdom.
Overall, this replication successfully reproduces the paper’s key empirical findings and illustrates why randomized assignment allows a credible causal interpretation.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Imagine you run a website and your landing page includes a spot where visitors can sign up for your newsletter. You’ve been using a single call to action (CTA) for months, but you wonder whether different wording might drive more sign-ups. You come up with two options:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

Which one performs better? Rather than relying on intuition, you decide to run an A/B test — a simple randomized experiment in which each visitor to your site is randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs. By comparing the sign-up rates between the two groups, you can make a data-driven decision about which wording is more effective.
Along the way, I use simulations to illustrate the Law of Large Numbers, the Central Limit Theorem, bootstrap uncertainty, hypothesis testing, the regression interpretation of difference-in-means, and the danger of peeking at experimental results too early.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Imagine you run a website and your landing page includes a spot where visitors can sign up for your newsletter. You’ve been using a single call to action (CTA) for months, but you wonder whether different wording might drive more sign-ups. You come up with two options:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

Which one performs better? Rather than relying on intuition, you decide to run an A/B test — a simple randomized experiment in which each visitor to your site is randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs. By comparing the sign-up rates between the two groups, you can make a data-driven decision about which wording is more effective.
Along the way, I use simulations to illustrate the Law of Large Numbers, the Central Limit Theorem, bootstrap uncertainty, hypothesis testing, the regression interpretation of difference-in-means, and the danger of peeking at experimental results too early.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Each visitor to our site either signs up or doesn’t, so we can model each visitor’s outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution. Let \(\pi\) denote the probability of signing up. Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence this probability, we allow the parameter to differ across groups: visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\), and those who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\).
The quantity we care about is the difference in sign-up rates:
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B
\]
If \(\theta > 0\), CTA A is more effective; if \(\theta < 0\), CTA B wins; and if \(\theta = 0\), there is no difference. Since we cannot observe \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\) directly, we estimate them from data. Our estimator of \(\theta\) is the difference in sample proportions:
\[
\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B
\]
Because each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean in each group is simply the proportion of visitors who signed up: \(\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B\).

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In the real world, we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\) — the whole point of running an experiment is to learn about them. But for this exercise, we set them ourselves so we can study how our estimator behaves when we know the ground truth.
Let us suppose \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), giving a true difference of \(\theta = 0.04\). We simulate 1,000 visitors in each group:

pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
theta_true = pi_A - pi_B
n = 1000

# Simulate 1,000 draws from each Bernoulli distribution
cta_a = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, size=n)
cta_b = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, size=n)

print(f"CTA A: {cta_a.sum()} sign-ups out of {n} visitors (sample proportion = {cta_a.mean():.3f})")
print(f"CTA B: {cta_b.sum()} sign-ups out of {n} visitors (sample proportion = {cta_b.mean():.3f})")
print(f"Estimated θ̂ = {cta_a.mean() - cta_b.mean():.4f}  (true θ = {theta_true})")

CTA A: 216 sign-ups out of 1000 visitors (sample proportion = 0.216)
CTA B: 178 sign-ups out of 1000 visitors (sample proportion = 0.178)
Estimated θ̂ = 0.0380  (true θ = 0.04000000000000001)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) tells us that as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. This is the foundation of our approach: it guarantees that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will get closer and closer to the true \(\theta\) as we collect more data.
To illustrate the Law of Large Numbers, we track the running sign-up rate in each group independently and then take their difference. As the number of visitors grows, the estimated difference in conversion rates stabilizes near the true treatment effect.

cummean_a = np.cumsum(cta_a) / np.arange(1, n + 1)
cummean_b = np.cumsum(cta_b) / np.arange(1, n + 1)
cumulative_diff = cummean_a - cummean_b






Difference between the running sign-up rates in groups A and B converging toward the true effect




The plot illustrates the LLN nicely. With only a handful of visitors, the running difference bounces around erratically — a few lucky or unlucky sign-ups can swing the estimate dramatically. But as the sample size grows, the estimate settles down and stabilizes near the true difference of 0.04. This is exactly why we need a large enough sample before drawing conclusions from an A/B test.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
Now that we trust our point estimate, the next question is: how precise is it? A single number like \(\hat\theta = 0.04\) is not very informative without some measure of uncertainty. This is where the bootstrap comes in.
The bootstrap is a resampling method that estimates the variability of a statistic without needing a closed-form formula. The idea is simple: we resample (with replacement) from our observed data many times, compute the statistic of interest on each resample, and use the standard deviation of those resampled statistics as our estimate of the standard error.

n_boot = 1000
boot_thetas = np.empty(n_boot)

for i in range(n_boot):
    boot_a = np.random.choice(cta_a, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_b = np.random.choice(cta_b, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_thetas[i] = boot_a.mean() - boot_b.mean()

boot_se = boot_thetas.std(ddof=1)

We can compare this to the analytical standard error for the difference in two independent Bernoulli proportions:
\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]

pi_a_hat = cta_a.mean()
pi_b_hat = cta_b.mean()
theta_hat = pi_a_hat - pi_b_hat

analytical_se = np.sqrt(pi_a_hat * (1 - pi_a_hat) / n + pi_b_hat * (1 - pi_b_hat) / n)

print(f"Bootstrap SE:   {boot_se:.5f}")
print(f"Analytical SE:  {analytical_se:.5f}")

Bootstrap SE:   0.01766
Analytical SE:  0.01777


The two standard errors are very close, confirming that the bootstrap provides a reliable estimate of uncertainty even without relying on analytical formulas.
Using the bootstrap standard error, we construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\):

ci_lower = theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper = theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

print(f"Point estimate θ̂ = {theta_hat:.4f}")
print(f"95% CI: [{ci_lower:.4f}, {ci_upper:.4f}]")

Point estimate θ̂ = 0.0380
95% CI: [0.0034, 0.0726]


In practice, this interval summarizes the range of effect sizes that are most consistent with the observed data under repeated sampling logic. If we were to repeat this experiment many times, about 95% of the resulting intervals would contain the true value of \(\theta\).

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is one of the most powerful results in statistics. It states that regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean (and therefore of our estimator \(\hat\theta\)) becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows.
To visualize this, we repeatedly draw fresh samples of varying sizes, compute \(\hat\theta\) each time, and plot the distribution of those estimates. We also overlay the theoretical Normal density implied by the CLT to see how well it fits:

sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
n_simulations = 1000
results = {}

for sz in sample_sizes:
    theta_estimates = np.empty(n_simulations)
    for i in range(n_simulations):
        a_draws = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, size=sz)
        b_draws = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, size=sz)
        theta_estimates[i] = a_draws.mean() - b_draws.mean()
    results[sz] = theta_estimates






Sampling distribution of θ̂ for different sample sizes, with theoretical Normal overlay




At small sample sizes (n = 25), the distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is rough, chunky, and somewhat irregular — the Bernoulli data can only produce a limited set of distinct sample proportions, so the histogram looks jagged and the Normal curve is a poor fit. As n increases, the histogram becomes progressively smoother and more bell-shaped, tracking the theoretical Normal overlay more closely. By n = 500, the distribution is nearly indistinguishable from the Normal curve. This is the CLT at work: it doesn’t matter that our underlying data are binary (0s and 1s); with enough observations, the sampling distribution of the mean difference is well-approximated by a Gaussian.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Now that the CLT has shown us that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples, we can perform a formal hypothesis test. We set up:

Null hypothesis: \(H_0: \theta = 0\) (the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate)
Alternative hypothesis: \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\) (the sign-up rates differ)

The goal is to assess whether the data provide enough evidence to reject \(H_0\).
To do this, we standardize our estimate. Under the null, \(\hat\theta\) has mean 0 and standard deviation \(SE(\hat\theta)\), so:
\[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]
Why do we standardize? Three steps of logic:

By the CLT, \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), its mean is 0.
Dividing a Normal random variable by its standard deviation yields a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). So under \(H_0\), \(z \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)\).
Because we know the distribution of \(z\) under the null, we can compute a p-value: the probability of observing a test statistic as extreme or more extreme than what we actually got, assuming \(H_0\) is true.

A note on terminology: this procedure is sometimes called a “t-test,” which can be a bit misleading. The classical t-test arises when data are drawn from a Normal distribution and the variance must be estimated — in that case the ratio follows an exact \(t\)-distribution. Here, our data are Bernoulli (not Normal), so there is no exact \(t\)-distribution result. What we rely on is the CLT’s approximate Normality, making this closer to a z-test. In practice, for large samples, the \(t\)-distribution and the standard Normal are nearly identical, so the distinction has little practical consequence — but it is important to understand the conceptual difference.
For confidence intervals, I used the estimated standard error based on the two separate sample proportions. For hypothesis testing under \(H_0: \pi_A = \pi_B\), the standard approach is to use the pooled proportion to compute the standard error, since the null assumes a common sign-up rate:

x_a = cta_a.sum()
x_b = cta_b.sum()
p_pool = (x_a + x_b) / (2 * n)

pooled_se = np.sqrt(p_pool * (1 - p_pool) * (1/n + 1/n))
z_stat = theta_hat / pooled_se
p_value = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))

print(f"θ̂ = {theta_hat:.4f}")
print(f"Pooled proportion under H₀ = {p_pool:.4f}")
print(f"Pooled SE under H₀ = {pooled_se:.5f}")
print(f"z-statistic = {z_stat:.4f}")
print(f"p-value = {p_value:.4f}")

θ̂ = 0.0380
Pooled proportion under H₀ = 0.1970
Pooled SE under H₀ = 0.01779
z-statistic = 2.1364
p-value = 0.0326




At α = 0.05, we reject the null hypothesis (p = 0.0326 < 0.05).
The data provide sufficient evidence that the two CTAs have different sign-up rates.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Difference-in-Means as a Regression
It turns out that the two-sample test we just performed is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. This connection is worth understanding because it means the same machinery — and the same software — that we use for regression can also perform hypothesis tests, and it generalizes naturally to more complex experimental designs with additional controls or treatment arms.
Here is the setup: we stack the CTA A and CTA B data into a single dataset with two columns — an outcome \(Y_i\) (1 if the visitor signed up, 0 otherwise) and an indicator \(D_i\) (1 if they saw CTA A, 0 if CTA B). Then we fit:
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]
The coefficients have clean interpretations:

\(\beta_0\) is the expected sign-up rate when \(D_i = 0\), i.e., the mean of the B group (\(\pi_B\)).
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected sign-up rate when \(D_i = 1\), i.e., the mean of the A group (\(\pi_A\)).
Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta\).

Because the outcome is binary, I report heteroskedasticity-robust (HC1) standard errors:

Y = np.concatenate([cta_a, cta_b])
D = np.concatenate([np.ones(n), np.zeros(n)])

X = sm.add_constant(D)
model = sm.OLS(Y, X).fit(cov_type="HC1")










Term
Estimate
Std. Error
t value
p value




0
Intercept (π_B)
0.178
0.01210
14.708
0.0000


1
CTA A indicator (θ)
0.038
0.01778
2.138
0.0325








print(f"\nRegression estimate of θ (β₁):  {model.params[1]:.4f}")
print(f"Two-sample estimate of θ̂:       {theta_hat:.4f}")
print(f"\nRegression robust SE of β₁:     {model.bse[1]:.5f}")
print(f"Analytical SE from earlier:      {analytical_se:.5f}")


Regression estimate of θ (β₁):  0.0380
Two-sample estimate of θ̂:       0.0380

Regression robust SE of β₁:     0.01778
Analytical SE from earlier:      0.01777


As expected, the regression coefficient \(\hat\beta_1\) matches our earlier estimate \(\hat\theta\) exactly. The robust standard error is very close to the analytical SE we computed earlier, since the HC1 correction accounts for the heteroskedasticity inherent in binary outcomes.
Why does this equivalence matter? Because the regression framework is far more flexible. If we later want to control for visitor demographics, test three CTAs instead of two, or add interaction terms, we simply expand the regression — the same approach carries through seamlessly.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting until all 1,000 visitors have been assigned to each group, she wants to check the results after every 100 visitors per group — peeking at \(n = 100, 200, 300, \ldots, 1000\). If any of those 10 checks comes back statistically significant at \(\alpha = 0.05\), she wants to declare a winner and stop the experiment early.
This is a common temptation, but it is deeply problematic under classical frequentist statistics. Here is why: a single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of producing a false positive (rejecting \(H_0\) when it is actually true). But each peek is another opportunity for a false alarm. Across 10 peeks, the probability of at least one false positive is much higher than 5%.
Let us demonstrate this with a simulation. We set up a world where the null hypothesis is true — both CTAs have the same sign-up rate (\(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\)) — and compare the false positive rate of a single final test versus repeated peeking.

single_test_fp = 0
peeking_fp = 0
n_experiments = 10000
peek_points = np.arange(100, 1100, 100)

for _ in range(n_experiments):
    a_draws = np.random.binomial(1, 0.20, size=1000)
    b_draws = np.random.binomial(1, 0.20, size=1000)

    # --- Single final test at n = 1000 ---
    p_a = a_draws.mean()
    p_b = b_draws.mean()
    p_pool = (a_draws.sum() + b_draws.sum()) / 2000
    se0 = np.sqrt(p_pool * (1 - p_pool) * (1/1000 + 1/1000))
    if se0 > 0:
        z = (p_a - p_b) / se0
        p_val = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z)))
        if p_val < 0.05:
            single_test_fp += 1

    # --- Repeated peeking at every 100 visitors ---
    any_sig = False
    for peek_n in peek_points:
        a_sample = a_draws[:peek_n]
        b_sample = b_draws[:peek_n]
        p_pool_peek = (a_sample.sum() + b_sample.sum()) / (2 * peek_n)
        se_peek = np.sqrt(p_pool_peek * (1 - p_pool_peek) * (1/peek_n + 1/peek_n))
        if se_peek > 0:
            z_peek = (a_sample.mean() - b_sample.mean()) / se_peek
            p_peek = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(z_peek)))
            if p_peek < 0.05:
                any_sig = True
                break
    if any_sig:
        peeking_fp += 1

print(f"False positive rate, single final test: {single_test_fp / n_experiments:.3f}")
print(f"False positive rate, repeated peeking:  {peeking_fp / n_experiments:.3f}")

False positive rate, single final test: 0.049
False positive rate, repeated peeking:  0.197


The result is striking. The single final test produces a false positive rate near the nominal 5%, as expected. But repeated peeking inflates it to roughly 15–20% — meaning that in a world where there is truly no difference between the two CTAs, we would incorrectly declare a winner about one in every five or six experiments.
This is why proper experimental design matters. If you want to peek at results during an experiment, you need to use methods specifically designed for sequential testing (such as group sequential designs or always-valid p-values) that control the overall Type I error rate across multiple looks. Simply running a standard hypothesis test at each checkpoint and stopping at the first significant result will fool you far more often than you might expect.

title: Ariel Yuan
text: Hi, I’m Ariel Yuan. Welcome to my website!
